fuzzy_prd: We need a bridge layer that makes compiled native functions callable from a dynamic scripting environment. When a developer exposes a native function through this bridge, callers in the dynamic environment should be able to pass arguments by position or by name, and any parameters not provided should fall back to their declared defaults. The bridge should also support exposing multiple implementations under the same name that differ by argument type — the runtime picks the right one automatically. If no implementation matches the argument, the bridge reports a clean type-mismatch error rather than crashing. Fixed-width integer types need explicit range checking: passing a value that doesn't fit the target size should fail with a type-mismatch, not silently wrap or truncate. Text and raw byte buffers both need to round-trip correctly across the bridge. Certain parameters can be declared as keyword-only following the same convention as the argument-binding layer uses for positional-only params, meaning the caller must name them explicitly and cannot accidentally pass them by position. Functions that handle open-ended argument lists should be able to capture any extra positional and keyword arguments beyond the declared parameters. When a native function throws an exception, that exception should propagate across the bridge as a runtime error carrying the original message, never exposing the host language's internal error type.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The subtract function has first parameter default=8, second parameter default=1. Called with no args: 8-1=7. Named parameters: first='i', second='k'. Mix example: args=[3], kwargs={'k':5} gives 3-5=-2.

trigger_keywords: how are overloads selected by type
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'classify' overload returns integer tag 1 for integer overload, 2 for real/float overload. Type dispatch is by genuine value type. A non-numeric type (e.g. string) produces 'error=type_mismatch'.

trigger_keywords: what is counted as extra positional args
declared parameters not counted in extras
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: count_extras: The first two positional args fill declared parameters and are NOT counted as extras. Additional positional args beyond the first two are counted in 'positional=<n>'. Any keyword arguments are counted in 'keyword=<n>'.

trigger_keywords: signed vs unsigned integer range limits
what is the max value for uint64
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Signed N-bit range: -2^(N-1) to 2^(N-1)-1. Unsigned N-bit range: 0 to 2^N-1. uint64 max = 18446744073709551615. Out-of-range values produce 'error=type_mismatch'. The full 64-bit unsigned range must round-trip exactly.

trigger_keywords: what error when positional for keyword-only
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: kw_only_all: Both parameters must be supplied by name (kwargs). Passing positionally produces 'error=type_mismatch'. kw_only_mixed: first parameter can be positional or keyword, second must always be keyword. Passing second positionally produces 'error=type_mismatch'.

trigger_keywords: what is the exception error format
how is native exception message reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Native exception propagation produces two lines: 'error=runtime_error\nmessage=<text>'. For raise_runtime, the message is 'oops!'. The host-language exception type is never exposed.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: following the same convention as the argument-binding layer uses for positional-only params
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 1 argument binding — nb::arg() declarations for positional parameters with defaults; keyword-only is declared via nb::kw_only() separator in the binding definition

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please check the start.md specification and the golden reference implementation for details.

feature1_default_keyword_args.json.description: A bound binary function over two integer parameters returns the first minus the second. The first parameter defaults to 8 and the second to 1 when omitted. Arguments may be supplied positionally, by name, in either name order, or as a mix; omitted trailing parameters fall back to their defaults. The output is the resulting integer.

feature2_type_overloading.json.description: A single callable name carries two overloads distinguished only by the runtime type of its one argument: one overload accepts an integer and one accepts a real (floating-point) number. The binding layer selects the overload by argument type and returns a small integer tag identifying which overload ran (1 for the integer overload, 2 for the real-number overload). When the argument matches no overload (for example, a text value), argument resolution fails and a neutral type-mismatch error is reported.

feature3_variadic_capture.json.description: A bound function declares two leading positional parameters and then captures any further arguments: extra positional arguments are gathered into a variadic positional pack, and any keyword arguments beyond the declared parameters are gathered into a variadic keyword pack. The function reports how many extra positional arguments and how many keyword arguments it received. Output lists the positional count and the keyword count, each on its own line.

feature4_integer_width_range.json.description: A family of identity functions each bound to a fixed-width integer type, parameterised by signedness (signed or unsigned) and bit width (8, 16, 32, or 64). When the supplied integer fits within the representable range of the chosen type it is returned unchanged; when it falls outside that range, argument conversion fails and a neutral type-mismatch error is reported. Signed N-bit types span -2^(N-1) .. 2^(N-1)-1; unsigned N-bit types span 0 .. 2^N-1.

feature5_1_string_roundtrip.json.description: A bound function accepts a text string and returns the same text, demonstrating round-trip marshalling of string values across the binding boundary. The output is the string unchanged, including any embedded punctuation or spaces.

feature5_2_byte_length.json.description: A bound function receives a raw byte buffer and returns its length in bytes. The buffer is supplied as an array of byte values (0-255). The length counts every byte, including embedded zero bytes, so a buffer of four zero bytes has length 4. Output is the integer length.

feature5_3_byte_prefix.json.description: A bound function builds a byte buffer from a text source but keeps only the first N bytes, where N is supplied explicitly. The result is the leading N-byte prefix of the source text. Output is that prefix rendered as text.

feature6_1_all_keyword_only.json.description: A bound function declares two integer parameters that are BOTH keyword-only: each must be supplied by name, in any order, and never positionally. Supplying them by name succeeds and the function returns the pair. Passing either value positionally, or omitting a required keyword, fails argument resolution and reports a neutral type-mismatch error. Output lists the two received values, each on its own line.

feature6_2_mixed_keyword_only.json.description: A bound function declares a leading parameter that may be passed positionally or by name, followed by a second parameter that is keyword-only. The first value may arrive either way; the second must always be a keyword. Mixing them validly returns the pair. Passing the keyword-only value positionally, or omitting it, fails argument resolution and reports a neutral type-mismatch error. Output lists the two received values, each on its own line.

feature7_exception_propagation.json.description: A bound function whose underlying native implementation unconditionally raises an exception. The binding layer propagates the failure across the boundary as a runtime error carrying the original diagnostic message. The adapter renders it as a neutral runtime-error category line followed by the preserved message.
